desired_outcome: Safe, juicy chicken cooked through without drying out.
failure_mode: Chicken breast becomes dry and tough when overcooked.
recovery_hint: Slice thinly and serve with sauce or gravy if it seems dry.
teaching_note: Resting helps the juices settle before slicing.
timing_note: Chicken breast needs attention while cooking and benefits from a short rest before slicing.
handling_note: Remove loose membrane and visible fat as desired. Do not rinse raw chicken. Pat dry and even the thickness before seasoning.
cooking_note: Heat a lightly oiled skillet over medium to medium-high heat. Brown the first side before turning. Continue cooking and check periodically.

desired_outcome: Tender grains that are fully cooked without becoming mushy.
failure_mode: Rice can scorch remain hard or become gummy when liquid and heat are poorly controlled.
recovery_hint: Add a small amount of hot liquid cover and continue gently if rice is still firm.
teaching_note: Rice should start early because most cooking time is passive and overlaps other work.
timing_note: Start rice early then let it cook mostly unattended while other components are prepared.
handling_note: Measure rice and liquid before heating.
cooking_note: Bring to a simmer cover reduce heat and cook without lifting the lid.

desired_outcome: Deeply browned mushrooms with concentrated savory flavor.
failure_mode: Crowded mushrooms steam and become pale instead of browning.
recovery_hint: Increase heat and let excess moisture evaporate before seasoning again.
teaching_note: Mushrooms benefit from browning before liquid is added.
timing_note: Brown mushrooms before adding liquid so they contribute fond and concentrated flavor.
handling_note: Quickly rinse attached dirt or wipe with a damp towel; do not soak. Trim dry stem ends and slice about 1/4 inch thick.
cooking_note: Heat a skillet over medium-high heat. Cook in a single uncrowded layer until moisture evaporates and the bottoms brown. Turn and finish cooking.

failure_mode: Asparagus becomes limp and dull when overcooked.
recovery_hint: Serve promptly and add a bright finishing seasoning if it softens too far.
teaching_note: Evenly sized pieces help asparagus cook at the same rate.
timing_note: Cook asparagus after sturdy components but before quick-wilting greens.
handling_note: Wash and remove woody ends. Leave spears whole or cut into bite-size pieces.
cooking_note: Heat oil or butter in a skillet. Sauté 2–3 minutes. Add a little water and cover briefly for thick stalks. Finish until bright green and crisp-tender.

desired_outcome: Bright tender greens that are wilted but not slimy.
failure_mode: Swiss chard becomes limp watery and unpleasant if overcooked or held too long.
recovery_hint: Drain excess liquid and season again before serving if it becomes watery.
teaching_note: Swiss chard cooks fast because its leaves collapse quickly under heat.
timing_note: Swiss chard wilts quickly and does not hold well so cook it near the end.
handling_note: Rinse leaves and stems thoroughly. Separate and slice edible stems. Leave leaves large or tear into manageable pieces.
cooking_note: Heat oil in a skillet. Cook sliced stems first until they begin to soften. Add leaves with a splash of liquid and toss until wilted and tender.
